text.title: AI Design Assistant Interaction Patterns
text.summary: Four interaction patterns for AI design assistants, selection rules, and failure modes for each.

list.item: Sidebar — always visible, context-aware suggestions, quick actions; best for real-time support during design work
Modal — focused generation tasks, complex operations, batch processing; best for one-shot generation requests
Inline — in-place suggestions, quick edits, micro-improvements on selected elements; best for property-level corrections
Review — audit and structured feedback against a rubric; best for pre-handoff quality gates
AI excels at: pattern recognition, variation generation, repetitive tasks, data processing
Humans excel at: creative direction, emotional understanding, brand judgment, strategic decisions

rule.statement: Choose one interaction pattern per tool; never mix patterns in the same UI surface.
rule.rationale: Mixing sidebar + modal + inline in one plugin creates navigation confusion. Users build mental models per pattern; switching patterns mid-workflow breaks them.

rule.statement: Always return structured JSON from the assistant, not prose, so the application layer can act on it programmatically.
rule.rationale: Prose output requires parsing before any programmatic action. Structured JSON with defined schema enables the application to filter, display, and log results without additional processing.

rule.statement: Cache assistant responses keyed on the design artifact hash to avoid redundant API calls when the design has not changed.
rule.rationale: Design files are read frequently but change less often. Caching eliminates repeated API calls for unchanged artifacts and reduces cost.

antipattern.reason: Sidebar assistants degrade when the user's working context changes faster than the context window updates. The assistant gives stale suggestions for the previous selection state.

antipattern.description: Review assistant without a confidence threshold filter.
antipattern.reason: Review assistants produce false positives at scale. Without a confidence threshold, noise overwhelms useful signal and designers stop trusting the tool.

antipattern.description: Documentation assistant on files with non-standard layer naming.
antipattern.reason: Documentation assistants hallucinate component descriptions when design files use non-descriptive layer names (e.g., "Frame 23", "Group 5"). Descriptive naming is a prerequisite for reliable doc generation.
